title: Grundschutz certification evidence pack

compliance.assurance_rationale: Auto-migrated from cat-22 markdown on Phase 1.3 run, then normalised in Phase A (clause 'IT-Grundschutz' → 'OPS.1.1.2'). Requires SME review to confirm clause accuracy, mode (satisfies vs detects-violation-of), and assurance level (contributing/partial/full).
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that IT-Grundschutz OPS.1.1.2 (Ordered ICT operation) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.28.15: Grundschutz certification evidence pack.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.28.15' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: Windows Security Event Logs, critical infrastructure system logs, BSI IT-Grundschutz compliance scans, SIEM correlation events, vulnerability scan outputs, change management records, incident response logs, security audit evidence
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621)
spl:
index=audit sourcetype="grundschutz:cert" earliest=-365d
| stats latest(expiry) as ex, latest(level) as lvl by scope_id
| eval ex_epoch=strptime(ex,"%Y-%m-%d")
| where ex_epoch<relative_time(now(),"+90d@d")
| table scope_id, lvl, ex
description: Demonstrates alignment with BSI IT-Grundschutz expectations for grundschutz certification evidence pack using auditable Splunk evidence.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for IT-Grundschutz compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes audit and sourcetypes grundschutz:cert. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Maintain `de_kritis_assets.csv` with sector classification and KRITIS identifiers. (2) Link ITSI services to KRITIS objects for business impact overlays. (3) Export monthly control posture snapshots for BSI engagement. (4) Tune thresholds with enterprise risk management and OT security owners. Indexes required: `index=audit`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=grundschutz:cert`.
visualization: Matrix (host x module), Bar chart (severity), Table (controls). Key fields: `ex`, `ex_epoch`, `lvl`, `scope_id`.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Vulnerabilities.Vulnerabilities by Vulnerabilities.severity, Vulnerabilities.signature, Vulnerabilities.dest | sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
grandmaExplanation: We watch for demonstrates alignment with bsi it-grundschutz expectations for grundschutz certification evidence pack using auditable splunk evidence. This is critical because gaps here could mean serious regulatory exposure or security blind spots.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Windows Security Event Logs, critical infrastructure system logs, BSI IT-Grundschutz compliance scans, SIEM correlation events, vulnerability scan outputs, change management records, incident response logs, security audit evidence.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Maintain `de_kritis_assets.csv` with sector classification and KRITIS identifiers. (2) Link ITSI services to KRITIS objects for business impact overlays. (3) Export monthly control posture snapshots for BSI engagement. (4) Tune thresholds with enterprise risk management and OT security owners.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=audit sourcetype="grundschutz:cert" earliest=-365d
| stats latest(expiry) as ex, latest(level) as lvl by scope_id
| eval ex_epoch=strptime(ex,"%Y-%m-%d")
| where ex_epoch<relative_time(now(),"+90d@d")
| table scope_id, lvl, ex
```

Understanding this SPL

**Grundschutz certification evidence pack (IT-Grundschutz)** — Demonstrates alignment with BSI IT-Grundschutz expectations for grundschutz certification evidence pack using auditable Splunk evidence.

Documented **Data sources**: Windows Security Event Logs, critical infrastructure system logs, BSI IT-Grundschutz compliance scans, SIEM correlation events, vulnerability scan outputs, change management records, incident response logs, security audit evidence. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: audit; **sourcetype**: grundschutz:cert. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=audit, sourcetype="grundschutz:cert", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` aggregates events by **scope_id**, producing one row per unique combination for trend analysis and threshold evaluation.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **ex_epoch** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Filters the current rows with `where ex_epoch<relative_time(now(),"+90d@d")` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Vulnerabilities.Vulnerabilities by Vulnerabilities.severity, Vulnerabilities.signature, Vulnerabilities.dest | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Grundschutz certification evidence pack (IT-Grundschutz)** — Demonstrates alignment with BSI IT-Grundschutz expectations for grundschutz certification evidence pack using auditable Splunk evidence.

Documented **Data sources**: Windows Security Event Logs, critical infrastructure system logs, BSI IT-Grundschutz compliance scans, SIEM correlation events, vulnerability scan outputs, change management records, incident response logs, security audit evidence. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Vulnerabilities.Vulnerabilities` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.

Output fields: ex, ex_epoch, lvl, scope_id.

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Matrix (host x module), Bar chart (severity), Table (controls).
